Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To Ccp Section 128.7; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Thereof; Declarations Of Ed Ivy, Reggie Lee And Craig A. Horowitz
Off Calendar Aug-14-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANTS AUTO-CHLOR SYSTEM OF NORTHERN CALIFORNIA, INC., AND AUTO-CHLOR SYSTEM, LLC's Motion For Sanctions Pursuant To Ccp Section 128.7.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendants Auto-Chlor System of Northern California, Inc. and Auto-Chlor System, LLC's motion for sanctions is denied.

Under California Code of Civil Procedure Section 128.7(b) and (c), the court may award sanctions if there is no demonstration that "[t]he allegations and other factual contentions have evidentiary support or, if specifically so identified, are likely to have evidentiary support after a reasonable opportunity for further investigation or discovery." Here, prior to discovery, the allegations that Auto-Chlor System, LLC was a joint employer or alter ago were not frivolous. The companies share a website, CEO, and employee handbook. A plaintiff could reasonably bring both of these claims with this information and think it possible that discovery may yield evidence that the corporation was in fact a joint employer or alter ego.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Aug-14-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT AUTO-CHLOR SYSTEM, LLC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Auto-Chlor System, LLC's motion for summary judgment is denied.

The operative complaint sues codefendant Auto-Chlor System of Northern California, Inc. and moving defendant Auto-Chlor LLC. It alleges that each was the agent and alter ego of the other.

On summary judgment, a moving party must present evidence. (Code Civ. Proc., section 437c, subd. (b)(1).) "Initially, the moving party bears a burden of production to make a prima facie showing of the nonexistence of any genuine issue of material fact. If he carries his burden of production, he causes a shift: the opposing party is then subjected to a burden of production of his own to make a prima facie showing of the existence of a genuine issue of material fact." (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 845.) The operative pleadings "delimit the scope of the issues" on which the moving party must present evidence. (Hutton v. Fidelity National Title Co. (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 486, 493 [citation simplified].) The moving party need not "refute liability on some theoretical possibility not included in the pleadings." (Id.) But it must present evidence on the theories alleged in the complaint. "[A] plaintiff who has pleaded a cause of action on either of two theories will not be subject to defeat by summary judgment because the defendant has established by an uncontradicted affidavit that [one] of the two theories (but not necessarily the other) cannot be established." (Cox v. State of California (1970) 3 Cal.App.3d 301, 310 [citation simplified].)

Auto-Chlor LLC's summary judgment motion presents evidence to carry its prima facie burden as the moving party on summary judgment as to plaintiff Vincent Williams's joint employer theory of liability, but it presents no evidence on Williams's alter ego theory. It therefore fails to carry its burden as the moving party on one of the two theories alleged and does not shift the burden of production to Williams.

Auto-Chlor LLC argues on reply that Williams's pleading of the alter ego is too thin to be countenanced. The court notes that it is pled in equal detail with the joint employer theory on which Auto-Chlor moved, and in addition in meet and confer correspondence Williams refused to dismiss Auto-Chlor LLC by relying in part on his alter ego theory. The court therefore does not see a notice problem; if Auto-Chlor LLC believed the theory was inadequately pled it could alternatively have moved for judgment on the pleadings on that theory. In any event, the court cannot in fairness to both parties consider a motion for judgment on the pleadings that is raised for the first time on reply.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)
